oakSays: Welcome to your Starter Lab! This is the workbench where we assemble your dream trio of starter Pokémon.
Tap this question mark any time to replay this guided tour, so no control ever stays a mystery for long.
This speaker turns the cheerful game sounds on or off, and your choice is remembered for next time.
These two buttons pick your art style, the polished FR-LG sprites or the vintage RBY look. It is purely cosmetic and never changes which team I pick.
Read this banner first, for it maps the whole flow: set your filters, spin the machine to reveal the top trio, then study the panels to learn why that team wins.
Generations is the master choice of the whole panel, the pool your dream trio is drawn from. Widen it or narrow it, and everything below reshapes around it.
This badge shows how many Pokémon are eligible right now, and it flashes when you change which generations are in play. A bigger pool gives me more trios to weigh.
Tap these region chips to choose where your starters come from, and you may light up more than one. I is Kanto, II is Johto, and III is Hoenn.
Once two regions are lit, this toggle decides how they blend. Any mix takes the strongest trio from anywhere, while Cross-gen forces one member from each region.
Pick a home town from a region you chose and I will only suggest starters you could catch nearby, or leave it on Anywhere to draw from the whole world.
Proximity sets how far from your base town I may wander, counted in route-steps along the roads. Each step opens the hunt a little wider for more species.
Filter by how hard a Pokémon is to find, because a dream team you can never catch is no team at all. Each step toward Common keeps that tier and everything easier.
The Types board holds one chip for every elemental type in its own color. Tap once to require that type in your trio, tap again to ban it, and a third tap clears it.
Here is the classic starter triangle of Fire, Water and Grass, where each beats the next in a friendly loop. Water is the gentlest first pick on defense.
For raw offense, lean on Electric, Ice, Fighting and Ground. Between them they answer Water, Flying, Dragon, Rock and more, so your trio always hits hard.
Want your trio to take a hit? Steel walls a great many types, Fairy slays Dragon and Dark, and Poison shrugs off plenty, so one of these keeps you standing.
The Evolutions row sets how far your starters can still grow, from Any for the widest pool to a single, two, or full three-stage line.
Flip Full-chain match on and your type and BST filters must hold across the WHOLE evolution line, not just the starting form. A stricter test for a team that stays on theme as it grows.
BST means Base Stat Total, the sum of a mon's six stats and a handy measure of raw power. This readout shows your current power window, updating live as you tune below.

This little row chooses which form the power window measures: the first stage, the second, the last, or the average across the line. It decides whether your limits judge the humble base form or the fully evolved champion.
These two sliders draw the floor and ceiling of that power window. Raise the lower one to demand stronger starters, or pull the upper one down for a plucky underdog team.
The Strategy menu picks the single rule I optimize for, and the whole ranking reshuffles to chase it, from a type triangle to widest coverage or fairest stats.
Combination size sets how many Pokémon make up the team. Tap 2 for a duo, 3 for the classic starter trio, or 6 for a full party.
This is the big one. Press Run optimizer and I search every eligible team, score them against your settings, and load the winners into the slot machine.
And Browse full dex opens the whole Pokédex, so you can study any Pokémon's types, stats and evolutions before you settle on a team.
One more handy tool up here. Copy link saves a web address that restores your exact settings, so you can bookmark a promising team or share it with a friend.
Welcome to your Starter Slots machine! This is where three reels spin up your trio, and the glowing sign and chasing bulbs are just cheerful arcade dressing.
These three windows are the heart of the machine, and each lands on a member of your trio. Before you spin they wear Poké Ball covers, and once revealed you can tap any reel for full stats.
And here is the SPIN button, the star of the show. Press it and watch me roll the reels to reveal your team, then use Prev and Next to walk through every ranked trio I found.

There they are! This shows which ranked team you are viewing, and the badge beside it names the strategy that landed it. The badge turns orange if I fell back to an easier goal, and a separate note appears only if I had to loosen your filters to find any team at all.

These four chips are my report card on your trio. Fairness grades how even the stat totals are, Coverage how many types you strike hard, BST spread the gap between strongest and weakest, and Triangle whether the three form a tidy cycle.
Here I show my thinking. The ladder lays out the goal I aimed for first and every fallback I climbed down to, marking the rung your team finally cleared.
The Type triangle draws your three starters as corners with arrows for who beats whom, so a closed golden loop means each one covers a teammate's weakness.
The Team readout lists your trio in full, each card showing its sprite, name, types and six base stats. Click any card to open the complete details.
This line names any types your trio cannot hit super-effectively, or cheers full coverage when none remain. Those gaps are the foes you would struggle to dent.
The Defensive profile flips to defense, one colored square per attacking type showing how your team holds up. Red squares warn of a shared weakness where two members fold to the same attack.
When a team can still grow, this bar appears. Press Evolve to march all three up a stage at once, or Devolve to step back, and watch how their stats and types shift as they mature.
Once you spin, your live Town Maps appear here, up to three side by side when your trio spans several regions. Each is the genuine in-game map with the route network drawn on top.
These pulsing green dots are the exact spots where your chosen three are caught in the wild. Hover one and it names the member waiting there.
Every town and route within your step limit glows red, so you can see at a glance how far you may roam from your base town to gather this trio.

And here is your key: green is a catch spot, red is a route or town within your step limit, and the pin is your base town. Kanto, Johto and Hoenn are all live, so go explore and build your dream team!
